name: Iaso
description: Iaso is the Greek goddess of cures, remedies, and modes of healing. She is one of the daughters of Asclepius, the god of medicine, and Epione, the goddess of the soothing of pain. Her sisters include Hygieia (goddess of health, cleanliness, and hygiene), Panacea (goddess of universal remedy), Aceso (goddess of the healing process), and Aglaea (goddess of beauty, splendor, glory, magnificence, and adornment). Iaso's role was primarily associated with the administration of cures and the practical application of healing knowledge. She was invoked alongside her father and sisters in prayers for health and recovery. While she doesn't have a wealth of independent myths, her importance is derived from her fundamental role within the Asclepiadae, the family of healers. Temples dedicated to Asclepius often included shrines or areas dedicated to his daughters, including Iaso, where suppliants would offer prayers and gifts in hopes of receiving divine assistance in their healing process. Iaso embodies the active application of medicine and the restoration of health through specific remedies and treatments, contrasting with her sisters who represent broader aspects of health and well-being. She is considered a vital component of the ancient Greek understanding of healing and the divine influence on health.
subtitle: Goddess of Cures and Remedies

keyMyths.title: The Asclepiadae's Healing Power
keyMyths.description: Iaso, along with her siblings, assisted Asclepius in his healing practices, each contributing their unique abilities to restore health to the sick. Iaso focused on the direct application of cures and remedies.

keyMyths.title: Invocation for Recovery
keyMyths.description: In ancient Greece, Iaso was invoked alongside Asclepius and her sisters by those seeking healing and recovery from illness or injury. Offerings were made at temples dedicated to Asclepius.

keyMyths.title: Role in Asclepieion
keyMyths.description: Within the Asclepieion, or healing temple, Iaso was honored as an integral part of the healing process. Her presence signified the active administration of cures.

sources.author: Pausanias
sources.description: A detailed account of ancient Greece, including descriptions of temples and deities.
sources.text: A detailed account of ancient Greece, including descriptions of temples and deities.

sources.author: Homeric Hymns
sources.description: A collection of ancient Greek hymns, including one dedicated to Asclepius and his family.
